topic: European digital speech & political content moderation (past week)

articles.headline_en: Shadow education secretary Bridget Phillipson deletes tweet accusing Rishi Sunak of ‘gaslighting’ over school budgets
articles.summary_en: The Guardian reports that UK Labour’s shadow education secretary Bridget Phillipson deleted a tweet in which she accused Prime Minister Rishi Sunak of “gaslighting” parents and teachers on school funding.[4] The post drew criticism from Conservative MPs, who said the language was inflammatory, and Labour sources later said the wording did not meet the party’s standards, prompting its removal.[4] The episode comes amid a heated political debate over education spending and cost‑of‑living pressures on schools.[4]
articles.significance: The incident illustrates how senior politicians are increasingly retracting or editing social media messages that use emotionally charged language, reflecting heightened scrutiny of tone and wording in online political debate.[4]

articles.headline_en: Spanish far-right Vox party deletes video accusing journalists of ‘being part of a criminal gang’ after backlash
articles.summary_en: According to El País, the far-right party Vox removed from its social media accounts a campaign video that described critical journalists as “part of a criminal gang” and accused them of conspiring against the party.[5] Media associations and opposition parties condemned the message as an attack on press freedom, and several reporters targeted in the clip threatened legal action, after which the video was quietly deleted.[5] Vox leaders defended their broader criticism of the media but declined to repost the footage.[5]
articles.significance: The case highlights tensions between populist parties and the press in Spain and shows how public outrage and potential legal exposure can force political actors to withdraw aggressive online content.[5]

articles.headline_en: French minister deletes post sharing misleading video of police confrontation after fact-checking dispute
articles.summary_en: Le Monde reports that a French junior minister deleted a post on X (formerly Twitter) in which he shared a cropped video of a police altercation during a protest and implied officers were under sudden attack.[6] Fact‑checking outlets and other users pointed out that the original, longer footage showed a more complex sequence of events, leading critics to accuse the minister of spreading disinformation; he removed the post and said he wished to “avoid further misunderstanding.”[6] The opposition has called for clearer rules on how public officials share protest footage online.[6]
articles.significance: This episode underscores concerns about public officials amplifying misleading clips on social media and the role of online fact‑checking in pressuring them to retract or correct such posts.[6]

articles.headline_en: German AfD politician deletes TikTok video calling refugees ‘invaders’ after investigation opened for incitement
articles.summary_en: Der Spiegel reports that a regional lawmaker from the far‑right Alternative for Germany (AfD) deleted a TikTok video in which he referred to refugees as “invaders” and urged supporters to “take back our streets.”[7] Prosecutors opened a preliminary investigation on suspicion of incitement to hatred, and TikTok users repeatedly reported the clip for violating the platform’s hate‑speech rules before the politician took it down.[7] AfD representatives framed the deletion as a response to “politically motivated censorship,” while prosecutors stressed the legal limits on inflammatory speech.[7]
articles.significance: The case shows how hate‑speech laws, platform rules, and user reporting can interact to constrain extreme rhetoric by elected officials on social media.[7]

articles.headline_en: Italy orders removal of Telegram channels doxxing magistrates and politicians after far-right harassment campaign
articles.summary_en: La Repubblica reports that an Italian court ordered the messaging service Telegram to remove several channels used by far‑right activists to publish private data and addresses of magistrates, journalists, and centre‑left politicians.[8] The channels had coordinated harassment and threats against judicial figures involved in extremist cases, prompting complaints to prosecutors and an urgent application to the court.[8] Telegram complied by blocking access to the identified channels for users in Italy, while investigators continue to examine other groups on the platform.[8]
articles.significance: The ruling reflects growing judicial willingness in Italy to require platforms to take down content that facilitates targeted harassment of public officials, raising questions about the balance between privacy, security, and online speech.[8]

articles.headline_en: Polish government pressures Meta to restore far-right Confederation party’s Facebook page ahead of local elections
articles.summary_en: Gazeta Wyborcza reports that the Polish government publicly criticized Meta after Facebook restricted a page associated with the far-right Confederation (Konfederacja) party for repeated violations of its hate‑speech policy.[9] The digital affairs minister sent a formal letter demanding an explanation and warning that new Polish rules could impose penalties on platforms seen as unfairly targeting political actors, while Meta said the enforcement was consistent with its global standards.[9] Opposition politicians accused the government of selectively defending accounts on its political flank while ignoring other moderation disputes.[9]
articles.significance: The dispute highlights how national governments are increasingly intervening in private platform moderation decisions when they affect politically significant actors, especially in the run‑up to elections.[9]

articles.headline_en: Spanish judge orders X (Twitter) to remove posts naming protected witness in terrorism case
articles.summary_en: La Vanguardia reports that an examining judge at Spain’s Audiencia Nacional ordered X (Twitter) to delete several posts that revealed the identity of a protected witness in an ongoing terrorism trial.[10] The judge held that the posts violated witness protection measures and posed a risk to the individual’s safety, instructing X to remove the content and provide data on the accounts that had shared it.[10] Civil liberties groups said they understood the need to protect witnesses but called for more transparency in how such removal orders are drafted and enforced.[10]
articles.significance: The order illustrates how courts are using existing legal tools to compel platforms to remove content that interferes with judicial proceedings, while civil liberties advocates push for safeguards against overreach.[10]

articles.headline_en: Irish media regulator Coimisiún na Meán launches investigation into YouTube over handling of political ads and disinformation
articles.summary_en: The Irish Times reports that Ireland’s new media regulator, Coimisiún na Meán, opened a formal probe into YouTube’s handling of political advertising and alleged disinformation in videos targeting Irish audiences.[11] The investigation, conducted under EU Digital Services Act (DSA) powers, focuses on whether YouTube has been sufficiently transparent about paid political messages and whether it responds adequately to notices regarding false claims about referendums and elections.[11] YouTube said it is cooperating and pointed to its existing policies on election integrity and content labelling.[11]
articles.significance: The case is one of the first high‑profile DSA‑based inquiries into a major platform’s political content policies, signalling more structured regulatory oversight of how platforms manage political speech in the EU.[11]

articles.headline_en: European Commission warns X (Twitter) over moderation of disinformation about EU institutions under the Digital Services Act
articles.summary_en: Politico Europe reports that the European Commission sent a formal request for information to X concerning its handling of disinformation and harassment targeting EU institutions and officials.[12] The Commission is examining whether X’s content moderation, labelling, and recommender systems comply with the obligations for very large online platforms under the EU’s Digital Services Act, particularly around political disinformation campaigns.[12] X has been given a deadline to respond with detailed data on takedown procedures, complaint handling, and algorithmic amplification.[12]
articles.significance: The move underscores the EU’s readiness to use DSA enforcement tools to scrutinise platform decisions that affect political discourse and public trust in EU institutions.[12]

articles.headline_en: Turkish authorities order blocking of social media posts critical of President Erdoğan after protests in Istanbul
articles.summary_en: Deutsche Welle reports that Turkey’s media regulator and courts ordered the removal or blocking of numerous social media posts and news articles criticizing President Recep Tayyip Erdoğan and the police response to protests in Istanbul.[13] Platforms were instructed to geo‑block the content in Turkey, and several journalists said they received notices that their posts would be inaccessible locally for allegedly insulting the president or inciting unrest.[13] Human rights groups condemned the measures as censorship, while Turkish officials defended them as necessary for public order and protection of the presidency’s dignity.[13]
articles.significance: Although Turkey is only partly in Europe, the case is relevant to the wider European debate on digital rights, illustrating extensive state use of legal tools to suppress online criticism of political leaders.[13]

editorial_overview: Across Europe over the past week, disputes over online political speech have centred on three main dynamics: politicians retracting inflammatory or misleading posts, courts and regulators ordering removals on legal grounds, and governments pressing platforms over moderation decisions. Cases from the UK, France, Germany, and Spain show public officials deleting posts or videos after fact‑checking, public backlash, or incitement investigations. Judicial and regulatory interventions in Italy, Spain, Ireland, and at EU level demonstrate a growing reliance on formal powers—especially under the Digital Services Act—to shape what political content stays online. At the same time, controversies in Poland and Turkey highlight governments’ increasing willingness to intervene when moderation affects allies or critics, raising concerns about selective pressure and censorship. Together, these developments suggest a rapidly tightening, but uneven, governance environment for digital political speech in and around Europe.
